response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a hairdresser living in Bologna, I've noticed how social media has become a breeding ground for fake content. It's everywhere - from exaggerated beauty standards to manipulated news stories. But, as someone with good mental health, I've learned to navigate these waters with a critical eye. Firstly, I try to limit my social media use to a few times a month, which helps me avoid getting caught up in the endless stream of misinformation. When I do log in, I make it a point to fact-check anything that seems suspicious or too good (or bad) to be true. I also try to engage with accounts that promote critical thinking and media literacy, which helps me stay informed and up-to-date on the latest trends and issues. Of course, it's not always easy - as someone with a high level of neuroticism, I can get anxious about the state of the world - but I've learned to take a step back and remind myself that most of what I see online is not real. By being more mindful and discerning in my online interactions, I'm able to manage the fake content that surrounds me and stay focused on what really matters. Whether it's a beautiful sunset or a good cup of coffee, I've learned to appreciate the simple things in life and not let the noise of social media get to me.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I recognize the prevalence of fake content on social media and its potential impact on my mental health. I limit my social media use to a few times a month and engage with critical thinking and media literacy accounts to stay informed. I fact-check suspicious information and try to maintain a critical eye when consuming online content. I remind myself that most online content is not real and that I should focus on appreciating the simple things in life.
